channel.title: Newest Python PEPs
channel.description: Newest Python Enhancement Proposals (PEPs): Information on new language features and some meta-information like release procedure and schedules.

item.title: PEP 840: Name Resolution in Class Namespaces
item.description: There is a long-standing inconsistency in the way variable names are resolved in classes. Several alternatives to resolve this inconsistency are discussed.
item.author: Jeremy Hylton (jeremy@python.org), Guido van Rossum (guido@python.org)

item.title: PEP 838: Adding python-version to pyvenv.cfg
item.description: This PEP proposes adding a python-version field to pyvenv.cfg which records the Python interpreter used by a virtual environment. It records only the major and minor version to be resilient to patch version updates.
item.author: Konstantin Schütze (konstin@mailbox.org)

item.title: PEP 837: Extensible JSON serialization
item.description: This PEP adds an extension mechanism to the json encoder consisting of three complementary parts, one per level of customization:
item.author: Serhiy Storchaka (storchaka@gmail.com)

item.title: PEP 836: JIT Go Brrr: The Path to a Supported JIT Compiler for CPython
item.description: The experimental Just-in-Time (JIT) compiler has been part of CPython’s main branch since Python 3.13. PEP 744 described part of its initial design and explicitly deferred a number of questions about the JIT’s long-term status. Since then, the JIT has been re-architected and matured considerably. In Python 3.15, it delivers a measurable, reproducible speedup over the interpreter (about 4-12% geometric mean performance improvement across measured Tier 1 platforms; see Appendix), emits frames that native debuggers can unwind through, and reduces the memory footprint of generated code relative to 3.14. Along the way, we have learned a good deal about what works for a JIT in CPython.
item.author: Savannah Ostrowski (savannah@python.org), Ken Jin (kenjin@python.org), Brandt Bucher (brandt@python.org)

item.title: PEP 835: Shorthand syntax for Annotated type metadata
item.description: This PEP proposes overloading the @ operator on types to allow writing Annotated[T, M] as T @M.
item.author: Till Varoquaux (till.varoquaux@gmail.com)

item.title: PEP 833: Freezing the HTML simple repository API
item.description: This PEP proposes freezing the standard HTML representation of the simple repository API, as originally specified in PEP 503 and updated over subsequent PEPs.
item.author: William Woodruff (william@yossarian.net)

item.title: PEP 829: Package Startup Configuration Files
item.description: This PEP changes the way packages influence Python’s startup process. Previously controlled through legacy .pth files parsed and executed by the site.py file during interpreter startup, such files are used to extend sys.path and execute package initialization code before control is passed to the first line of user code.
item.author: Barry Warsaw (barry@python.org)

item.title: PEP 830: Add timestamps to exceptions and tracebacks
item.description: This PEP adds an optional __timestamp_ns__ attribute to BaseException that records when the exception was instantiated with no observable overhead. When enabled via environment variable or command-line flag, formatted tracebacks display this timestamp alongside the exception message.
item.author: Gregory P. Smith (greg@krypto.org)

item.title: PEP 831: Frame Pointers Everywhere: Enabling System-Level Observability for Python
PEP 828: Supporting 'yield from' in asynchronous generators
item.description: This PEP introduces support for yield from in an asynchronous generator function:
item.author: Peter Bierma (peter@python.org)
